speaker: interviewer
text: W-E-I-S-S. This is roll 9.

speaker: interviewer
text: Robert, we've heard a fascinating history from you about your experiences during the war. And now I would like you to tell us some of your feelings about these experiences and some of your attitudes, emotions, et cetera. Would you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I want to say that I feel I was very lucky. I was some times and a certain place at the certain time when everything went right and didn't go wrong. When Ernst Weiss, which was in the Vienna [GERMAN] in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [GERMAN] Department, told me, Bob, I think you should go and ask, as a volunteer, to work rather than they pick you up. You should do it. And I took his advice, and I did it. And this-- and that way I had to work in Vienna instead of the labor camps where I could never escape anymore. And when I went to Aron Menczer and I say, look, help me. I left-- I-- I am without a home now. And also, I have the notification. I have to go to Poland. Please help me. He did help me. He showed me the way and the means to go to-- to escape to Yugoslavia. And I deeply regret that he himself, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the person which opened up the way for hundred other-- hundreds of other people to escape, that he didn't escaped, that he rather chose to go with the-- to the-- with the remainder. And it was to his-- to his death. When I came to Zagreb, I was so fortunate to meet up with Joschko. He was the right person [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to have the foresight and the guts to organize an escape route. First of all, he organized an escape route over the Danube for 100 children to go to Palestine. But when this route closed, he had the fortitude to take the rest of the children. He didn't abandon them. He could have abandoned them. He would-- it would have been a much easier way for him. No, he didn't. He took those children, including my mother and Mr. [? Arvien, ?] the cook, and all the others, and for years he traveled with us from station to station, and he guided us. Not only he made us survive physically, he made us grow mentally, which is a tremendous thing. For me to have found people on this route, on this way, ordinary people, some of them Jewish, some of them non-Jewish, which did help me without knowing me as a person, and two brothers Bolaffi in Ljubljana, rich wine merchant, which gave all their money to help other Jews. I saw many other Jews which turned all-- which turned their backs. But some people really helped us. And I saw it all over. I saw priests which helped us, Italian poor farmers, which had nothing in material goods. But whatever they had and food or-- they did share it with us, and they helped us. So I feel very, very fortunate. And of course, Palestine, and later Israel, meant a lot to me and still does. And I came to the United States and I married. And I have two-- two boys, which grow up, and one is carrying my name, and one is carrying my father's name. And I'm very happy. I have a grandchild, a boy child, and my name will continue. And my-- and the name is very important for me, because a large part of my family doesn't exist anymore. And I'm very fortunate that all through my life I was able to make it and to have a good life out of it. And later on, I wanted to leave something. So I started to write my memories and the memories of the group in a book form, thinking that when my grand-grandchildren grow up, they would like, perhaps, to know what grand-daddy did during those years. And I would like to have names preserved, because I feel they have the right to. I have the obligation to do that. And I'm happy that this came about, and I can say my little sayings. And I want to tell you another thought of mine. I know a lot of people didn't make it. Thousands died, but a few people survived. And we have children now, and they will have children. And I think that we are better now than we were before. We grew with that, at least I did. That's more [? what I-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any thoughts or comments on the Shoah itself, on the destruction, on the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Shoah, there was always a Shoah. That was only the latest one. And I had the fortune, or misfortune, to-- to live through the time. But let's not forget in Jewish history, when we look back, there was Shoah after Shoah after Shoah. And if we look forward, there's nothing what we could foresee, but there may be another Shoah in the future, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Shoahs are nothing new. And not only in Jewish history are Shoahs, in every history is Shoahs. Look what happened in Yugoslavia right now. Do you think that the people are different? Look what happened in Ireland. Look what happened in Africa. Look what happened in the United States with the Indians. Shoahs? There was always Shoahs. The-- the anti-Semitism that existed, exists perhaps, in Austria-- let's speak of Austria alone because you know it the best. Where do you think it came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism in-- in Austria, as well as in-- in the Western part of Europe, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when uneducated poor people are told by some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by some people, and mostly, if I look back now a couple of hundred years, by the Church, that something happened, like Christian blood is spilled, they rebel. If they see that somebody has a little bit more than they have, they-- they have a jealousy. They have an-- an-- and if it is easy, because those people have no rights and no weapons and no chance to defend themself, it's easy to kill and easy to steal. That happens through all the years. Austria, in particular, because it is an almost 100% [? Catholic ?] country, because there was a cardinal by the name of Innitzer, which greeted the-- the German troops and Hitler with the-- with the [? Hitlergruss, ?] when they saw how the Church didn't speak up, when they saw it is easy to steal and to enrich themselves, they did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how can you explain Italy, which is the capital of Catholicism, 110% Catholic country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Italy had a pope, which in the late part of 1943, just after the putsch of [? Badoglio, ?] gave out a bulla. A bulla is an document to-- to-- to his church, to his priests, and [INAUDIBLE]. And this bulla said change. Help the Jews. And from that moment on, not all of them, but some of the priests helped the Jews. The pope, up to this time, never, and I emphasize never, said anything to help the Jews. In matter of fact, he was only interested to help-- to have the Catholic Church in Austria or in Germany sustained. Because Hitler was against the Catholics, too, against the Church. So in the moment the pope changed, a little bit that changed, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you are a a very well-read, intelligent, highly experienced man. And we're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --grateful that you are here--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with us. And we thank you-- your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. That's a picture of my father, my mother, my brother Victor, and myself taken in Vienna in 1928. This is my-- this is my friend Aron Menczer. He was the madrich in Vienna in the [? Aliya. ?] And unfortunately, although he helped many, many people, he choose to go with the last group. And the group, and he himself, didn't survive. When we had to go into hiding in Nonantola, Don Arrigo and Dr. [? Beccari-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --supplied us with identification papers, which were falsified. And this is the identification paper for my mother, Josephina Weiss. And this is my identification paper. And both of them were later on imprisoned when the Germans found out that they made out those papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you here about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- perhaps I was around 17 at the time, perhaps 17 and 1/2. This is a hand-drawn map from the route we took, and it was made by Joschko Indig. And the only part which is not on the map is the way from Nonantola to Switzerland. This is a picture of the Villa Emma. And in front you see also a part of the vegetable garden we had there. This is a picture of our children, and they're mostly our younger ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this Villa Emma or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Villa Emma, yeah, taken in the time of Villa Emma. That's a picture of my Swiss Nansen pass. And it was made out to-- to facilitate our legal entry from Switzerland into Palestine. The pass is is only valid for the exit out of Switzerland and not anymore for an return. On top you have the Palestine certificate, a very rare occasion that somebody got an certificate, and also the exit visa from Switzerland. And a few years back, six members of the group met in Connecticut, and we had an joyful reunion. All of those six members are living right now in the United States.
